title: Investigation of Electrical Double Layers on SIO2 Surfaces by Means of Forcevs. Distance Measurements
abstract:
SURFACE AND INTERFACE ANALYSIS, VOL. 25, 543È547 (1997)
Investigation of Electrical Double Layers on SiO2
Surfaces by Means of Force vs. Distance
Measurements
Grit Hu ttl,* Dirk Beyer and Eberhard Mu ller
Freiberg University of Mining and Technology, Institute for Ceramic Materials, Gustav-Zeuner Str. 3, D-09596 Freiberg,
Germany
The formation of electrical charges at solid/liquid interfaces results in a di†use electrical double layer close to the
solid surface. This layer determines, for example, the behaviour of ceramic particles in aqueous media and the
e†ective pore radius of membranes acting in liquids. The
system is used to demonstrate that linear
SiO2/SiO2
measurements using atomic force microscopy are suited for characterizing double layers at real surfaces. It has to
be taken into consideration that the tip itself, which is acting as the probe, is also surrounded by a double layer and
responds to the composition of the electrolyte in a material-speciÐc way. For this reason an oxidized silicon tip was
used for the present investigations, so that tip and sample consisted the same material. In this way it is possible to
obtain results that can be evaluated easily and compared with the DLVO theory.

KEYWORDS: AFM; force measurement; liquid cell;
tip modiÐcation
SiO2 ;
INTRODUCTION
If a ceramic surface is in contact with a polar liquid (e.g.
water), an electrically charged layer is formed at the
solid/liquid interface by di†erent mechanisms.1 The
properties of the layer depend on the charge of the solid
surface, the composition of the electrolyte (pH, salt
concentration) and the dipole momentum of the liquid
molecules. This so-called electrical double layer plays an
important role in colloidal processes. The stability and
rheological
behaviour
of
colloidal
suspensions,
for
example, depend on double layer forces between par-
ticles dispersed in liquids. Therefore, many investiga-
tions on surface forces in aqueous media focus on
identifying the inÑuence of technologically relevant
parameters on the behaviour of the double layer.
In the case of force measurements at real surfaces, an
atomic force microscope2 is superior to other devices,
e.g. the Israelachvili Surface Forces Apparatus,3 because
of the relatively small interacting area. For atomic force
microscopy (AFM) investigations, this interacting area
must be smooth and its size comparable to the tip
radius.4
* Correspondence to: Grit Hu ttl, Freiberg University of Mining
and Technology, Institute for Ceramic Materials, Gustav-Zeuner Str.
3, D-09596 Freiberg, Germany.
Contract grant sponsor: Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft; grant
no. SFB 285.
The tip is relatively easily modiÐable, e.g. by sticking
on particles or by coating processes, so that a variety of
speciÐc investigations are possible. For characterizing
the double layer, the tip acts in the same way as the
sample does, because in water it is also surrounded by
an electrical double layer whose properties depend on
the tip material.
There are di†erent ways in which speciÐc results can
be achieved simply by AFM:
(1) Using a tip made of a conductive material with a
deÐned potential, so that the dielectric properties of
the tip are determined exactly and its behaviour is
independent of the electrolyte composition.
(2) Using
sample
and
tip
consisting
of
the
same
material so that they respond in the same way to a
change in electrolyte composition and thus, presum-
ably have the same surface potential.
The second method is discussed here. The relationships
can be investigated easily using tip/sample systems that
are based on the composition of commercially available
tips (silicon, silicon nitride). For this reason,
is
SiO2
chosen as substrate.
THEORETICAL CONSIDERATIONS
The mechanism of stability of lyophobic colloids is
described by the Derjaguin, Landau, Verwey and Over-
beek theory (DLVO).5 The problem is treated by the

(or energies). The addition of the distance-dependent
functions of repulsive force (electrostatic force) and
attractive force (van-der-Waals force) results in the total
force function

tion and the sum of van-der-Waals interactions (for a

The Debye length (see Eqn (3), reciprocal value of the
DebyeÈHu ckel parameter6) is a measure of the thick-
ness of di†use double layers, i.e. at the distance of the

of di†erent ions i, as well as by the temperature of the
electrolyte. The considerations of this paper will focus
on water, so that the inÑuence of the relative permit-
tivity of the electrolyte remains unchanged.
Usually, the potential of the double layer of dispersed
particles is investigated by electrokinetic e†ects. In the
present case the measurement of electrokinetic sonic
amplitude (ESA) was applied. During the process, the
particles surrounded by a double layer are accelerated
by an external high-frequency alternating electromag-
netic Ðeld. Due to the resulting oscillating motion of
particles, the counter-ions of the electrical double layer
are almost completely stripped o†. Each solid particle is
no longer electrically neutral on a microscopic scale,
but has a net charge measured as the Zeta potential.
The ESA measurements with some oxides (Fig. 1)
revealed that the Zeta potential behaves di†erently from
oxide to oxide, depending on the pH. The isoelectric
points, i.e. the pH value at which the Zeta potential is
equal to zero, are quite di†erent.
The surface charge of many oxides dispersed in water
is determined by the association/dissociation action of
protons with hydroxyl groups at the surface. Thus H`
and OH~ are the potential determining ions and the
surface potential is a function of, among others, pH and
the surface composition. If the surface compositions of
tip and sample are di†erent, their electrical double
layers respond in a di†erent way to the change of pH of
electrolyte.
For this reason it is difficult to analyse the inÑuence
of pH on force measurements when di†erent materials
are used for tip and sample. Provided that the measure-
ments are conducted using identical materials for tip
and sample, easily analysable results can be obtained.
These results are comparable with values of the DLVO
theory, because we can assume that both parts (tip and
sample) give the same contribution to the measured
signal. An equation describing the electrostatic force
between chemically di†erent materials is given by Butt.7
However, for evaluating the inÑuence of pH on the
measurements, a comparative experiment with a well-
known sample has to be carried out.8
Hitherto, existing papers in this Ðeld have mainly
dealt with the inÑuence of salt concentration on the
thickness of double layers (Si3N4/Mica,9 Si3N4/Si3N4,10
between polystyrene spheres11). In these cases the
experiments were conducted at constant pH. The results
revealed
a
compression
of
the
double
layer
with
decreasing salt concentration, as was to be expected
according to Eqn (3). The measurements easily analys-
able because the pH was kept constant.
Figure 1. Electrokinetic sonic amplitude measurement as a function of pH using different oxides.

ELECTRICAL DOUBLE LAYERS ON SiO2 SURFACES
Measurements were carried out using fused quartz
metic mean deviation of the proÐle, according to the
data of the product) as sampling material and an oxi-
dized silicon tip prepared as described below.
Other scientists modiÐed the tip by sticking a spher-
ical particle of several micrometers in diameter on the
end of the tip (or cantilever).9,11 This facilitates the
selection of a speciÐc material of an accurately deÐned
tip radius. Another option for producing a tip with
deÐned properties is by direct modiÐcation of its
surface, e.g. by coating or a chemical process.
is preferred because silicon tips are commercially avail-
layer on the tip used by us was prepared
by an oxidation process at 1000 ¡C under normal atmo-
sphere (muffle furnace). The heat treatment of the silicon
tip (150 min) resulted in the formation of an oxide layer
of some 100 nm. As shown in Fig. 2, the tip can be
assumed to be almost exactly spherical.
increases from 10 to 400 nm for the present experi-
ments. In the present state of the experiments we had to
accept such an increase in tip radius because we wanted
to be sure that a sufficiently thick oxide layer is formed.
Further experiments will show if thinner oxide layers
will give comparable results. But by using this method
we are sure that, the e†ective radius of our tip is still
essentially smaller than the radius that is realizable with
a stuck-on particle. A smaller tip has a smaller inter-
action area with the sample surface. We obtain more
reliable results that depend less strongly on random
inÑuences of surface roughness. It is enormously impor-
tant for measurements on real surfaces. Another advan-
tage of our method is the absence of any glue, which
can inÑuence the properties of the solution.
The force measurements were carried out with a Dis-
coverer (Topometrix) atomic force microscope using a
liquid cell. A cycle of measurements was started at a
large tipÈsample distance, i.e. without any interaction
between them, so that the cantilever was not deÑected.
When approaching the tip and sample towards each
other, the cantilever was deÑected by the surface forces.
After the tip and the sample have been in contact, the
tip is retracted. During the whole cycle the deÑection of
the cantilever was recorded as a function of the dis-
tance. The resulting graph was converted into a force vs.
distance curve, which is independent of the spring con-
stant, using a method published by Butt et al.4 The
measurements were performed with a background of
10~3 M KCl (produced from high-purity water saturat-
conductivity at 25 ¡C \0.8 lS cm~1) at
di†erent pH. The pH was adjusted with HCl and KOH.
By using 10~3 M KCl as background, the inÑuence of
ions necessary to adjust the pH can be neglected and
therefore it can be considered as a constant ionic
strength. For obtaining reproducible measurements,
fused quartz was heat treated at 900 ¡C to remove pol-
Every series of experiments was started in the strong
acidic pH range because the chemical resistance of fused
quartz is worse in the presence of OH~. The pH was
checked prior and after each measurement.
The results of the experiments are shown in Fig. 3. The
spring constant of the cantilever used was 0.19 N m~1.
Due to the low spring constant, a high measuring sensi-
tivity was reached. When selecting an appropriate tip a
compromise has to be made between a cantilever with a
low spring constant, resulting in a high measuring sensi-
tivity, and one with a high spring constant for increased
Because the results are also inÑuenced by the dura-
tion of measurements, this problem is brieÑy discussed
Figure 2. The SEM image of an oxidized silicon tip.
( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
G. HU TTL, D. BEYER AND E. MU LLER
Figure 3. Force vs. distance curves using an oxidized silicon tip
and a plane-fused glass surface with varying pH. Only the
in the following. On the one hand, the measurement
should be as fast as possible to avoid corrosion of the
surface as a potential source of error, especially at
basic pH. On the other hand, a certain time interval is
necessary for the stabilization of relaxation in order to
obtain reproducible measurements. For this reason, the
procedure based on the experiences with ESA measure-
ments was adopted and a relaxation time of 40 s after
changing the pH was chosen, which is considered the
minimum time interval between two subsequent mea-
surements. After the pH was changed, a certain time is
necessary until the force measurements show repro-
ducible results; in the present case, this time interval
When force vs. distance curves are recorded as a func-
tion of pH, a qualitative consistency of these curves
noticed. At pH 4, which is near the isoelectric point, no
repulsive components are present. If the pH reaches a
value of 6, both electrostatic repulsion and van-der-
Waals attraction are recognized. At pH 8, the repulsion,
by overlapping of double layers, dominates over the
whole range of interaction. When the pH is increased
further (up to 10), the interacting energy raises analo-
gously with the amount of Zeta potential when the sur-
faces are in contact. This conclusion can only be drawn
on the assumption that no other parameters are
changed with the exception of pH.
The results demonstrate that the method proposed
hereÈthe use of an oxidized tipÈis well suited for
investigating the double layer on the surface of silicon
oxide (tip and sample of identical composition). The
results are reproducible, although the roughness of the
sample was relatively high in comparison to the oxi-
dized silicon wafer used in Ref. 12. This is in agreement
with our statement mentioned above, that a smaller tip
is more suitable for investigations of a real surface.
More local information can thus be obtained that is not
averaged to such a degree by a relatively large sphere
The objective of the experiments was a Ðrst trial of
the method proposed here. For testing the agreement
with DLVO theory, further measurements have to be
done by changing further parameters (concentration,
